stories.title: Integra Investments Buys West Palm Marina For $72M
stories.excerpt: The Huizenga family has sold a marina in West Palm Beach, Fla., to Integra Investments for $72.4 million, property records show. The asset, called Safe Harbor Rybovich, spans 15 acres at 4200 North Flagler Drive, between 38th and 44th streets, facing the Intracoastal Waterway. Last year, Huizenga Holdings filed plans to build a 2.6 million-square-foot, mixed-use development at the site, which officials approved. The proposal calls for the construction of four condo towers with 660 units in total as well as 20,691 square feet of office space, 37,445 square feet of retail, 14,376 square feet of restaurants, 12,750 square feet of crew amenities and 3,355 square feet of marine storage. Upland Debt Lender provided a $75 million floating-rate loan to multiple affiliates backed by different components of the future development, including a marina, retail space, condominiums and a parking garage. The project marks the first in West Palm Beach for Integra, a prominent Miami-based developer, and the latest for the city’s waterfront. Just north of the marina, Related Group completed the Icon Marina Village luxury rental complex in 2023. Other luxury condo projects in the pipeline include Related Ross’s Edgeworth, Shorecrest and South Flagler House, the last of which remains under construction, as well as Savanna’s Olara. Huizenga Holdings, led by H. Wayne Huizenga Jr., manages the Huizenga family’s private businesses and real estate. Late patriarch Wayne Huizenga founded AutoNation, among other companies, and had owned the Miami Dolphins and the Florida Panthers sports franchises. A representative for Huizenga Holdings could not be reached for comment, while a spokesperson for Integra has yet to provide comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Integra Investments acquires Safe Harbor Rybovich marina, West Palm Beach, for $72.4M
Approved mixed-use plan: 660 condo units across four towers, 75K SF retail/restaurant/office
$75M floating-rate construction loan from Upland Debt backs marina, retail, condo, parking components
stories.summary: Integra Investments bought the Huizenga family's 15-acre marina in West Palm Beach for $72.4M, unlocking a previously approved mixed-use redevelopment with 660 residential units across four condo towers, plus 75,000 SF of retail, restaurant, office and marine space. The deal is Integra's first West Palm Beach project and marks another waterfront activation in a competitive South Florida market. A $75M floating-rate construction loan from Upland Debt finances multiple project components.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach waterfront is consolidating around high-density residential+retail mixed-use, signaling strong demand for upscale urban condo product in the submarket. Approval speed and financing availability (floating-rate debt) indicate lender confidence in South Florida multifamily; watch for condo-to-rental conversion pressure if market softens post-delivery.

stories.title: GO Residential REIT Completes Brooklyn Apartment Building Acquisition
stories.excerpt: GO Residential REIT has been on a bit of a buying spree this year, and it just completed its previously announced acquisition of an approximately 81 percent managing interest in a multifamily building in Crown Heights, Brooklyn. The real estate investment trust, using the LLC GO 409, acquired control of the 186-unit residential building at 409 Eastern Parkway for $109 million, according to property records made public Monday. GO purchased the stake from a trio of sellers: FBE Limited, Adam America Real Estate and Zev Marmurstein. Adam America will retain a 19 percent stake in the property. The deal, first announced in March, closed July 1 and was recorded July 20, records show. The property has 197,395 square feet of built space and three ground-floor retail units. PincusCo first reported the sale. Current retail tenants at the 11-story building include restaurant Cornbread Brooklyn and physical therapy clinic JAG Physical Therapy. The property has a tax exemption status under the now-sunsetted 421-a program, PincusCo noted. The tax exemption status took effect in 2020, and will expire in 2056. Max Kaufman, chief operating officer at GO, signed the deal for the buyer, records show. Yehoshua Fruchthandler, a managing member at FBE Limited, Florin Radu, chief commercial officer at Adam America, and Marmurstein signed the deal for the sellers. GO did not immediately respond to a request for comment, while the sellers could not be reached for comment. The buyer has been busy scooping up residential properties around the city. Earlier this year, GO acquired 350 West 43rd Street — also known as Ivy Tower — from Friedman Management, as well as 411 West 35th Street and 444 West 35th Street from Maddd Equities, Commercial Observer reported in February. The firm acquired the properties for a total of $380.5 million. It’s also in contract to acquire the 209-unit residential portion of 7 Dey Street in the Financial District from SL Green Realty. Following GO’s acquisition in Cr
bullets: GO Residential REIT acquires 81% stake in 186-unit Crown Heights building for $109M
Property carries 421-a tax exemption (effective 2020, sunsetted status); three retail tenants on ground floor
Deal closed July 1, 2026; retained co-owner Adam America holds 19% minority stake
stories.summary: GO Residential REIT completed a $109M acquisition of an 81% managing interest in a 186-unit, Class A garden-style building at 409 Eastern Parkway in Crown Heights, Brooklyn. The property includes 197,395 SF across 11 stories, three ground-floor retail units (Cornbread Brooklyn, JAG Physical Therapy), and benefits from a 421-a tax exemption (sunsetted). Adam America Real Estate retained a 19% minority stake.
stories.impact: Crown Heights multifamily trading at ~$585K/unit suggests Brooklyn outer-borough stabilized assets command strong REIT acquisition appetite. Loss of 421-a abatement at expiration will pressure NOI on expiring-exemption buildings citywide; GO's willingness to buy into sunsetting exemption signals confidence in rental growth to absorb tax increases.

stories.title: Celina Developer Moving Forward on 55K-SF Mixed-Use Venture
stories.excerpt: Nack Development is moving forward with a 55,000-square-foot mixed-use project in Celina, with the help of a $3 million incentive package. Trackside Junction will include retail, restaurant, office, event and residential space. Community Impact reports that the plans for the two buildings include a four-story, 40,000-square-foot mixed-use building with 10,000 square feet of retail and restaurants on the main floor and three floors of offices. The second building will include 15,000 square feet, with 5,000 square feet of retail and restaurants on the main floor and residential above it. The incentive package includes the city selling a $1 million parcel of land to Nack for $1, a $1.7 million tax increment reinvestment zone contribution and a $350,000 grant. In return, Nack invests at least $15 million in the project, breaks ground by the first of next year, completes both buildings for mid-2028 and pays for some traffic improvements. The post Celina Developer Moving Forward on 55K-SF Mixed-Use Venture appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nack Development advances Trackside Junction (55K SF) in Celina with $3M incentive package
Two-building plan: 40K SF office/retail (four-story) + 15K SF mixed-use; $15M private investment required
City concessions: $1M land discount, $1.7M tax-increment reinvestment zone (TIRZ), $350K grant; completion target mid-2028
stories.summary: Nack Development is moving forward on Trackside Junction, a 55,000-SF mixed-use project in Celina, Texas, supported by a $3M public incentive package (land discount, TIRZ contribution, grant). The two-building development features a 40,000-SF office/retail tower and a 15,000-SF mixed-use building with residential above retail. Total private investment is $15M+, with completion targeted for mid-2028.
stories.impact: Celina is a fast-growing Dallas suburb increasingly attracting mixed-use infill; subsidized office+retail+residential suggests municipal strategy to build downtown walkability ahead of suburban population growth. TIRZ financing and land deals signal cities are prioritizing downtown activation to compete for young professionals and reduce sprawl—favorable tailwind for attached multifamily in town centers.

stories.title: PCCP Provides $51.8 Million Refi of Gilbert 236-Unit Multifamily Community
stories.excerpt: PCCP has provided a $51.8 million senior loan to a joint venture between Phoenix Capital Management and P.B. Bell for the refinance of Everly at Morrison Ranch, located at 4353 E. Elliot Road in Gilbert. Built in 2025, the 236-unit Class A garden-style multifamily community is 97% leased and is within Morrison Ranch, a master-planned neighborhood in Gilbert. Everly Morrison Ranch includes 36 two-story buildings with an average unit size of 986 square feet. The unit mix includes one-bedroom (31%), two-bedroom (57%), and three-bedroom (12%) floor plans. Outdoor amenities will feature a pool and spa with poolside cabanas, a ramada, barbecue grills, swinging sofa beds, a dog park, gaming areas featuring cornhole, horseshoe and giant chess and a neighborhood-themed tot lot. Indoor amenities include a 24-hour Bulk Up Barn fitness center, dog spa, and clubhouse featuring private work and conference spaces, gaming and entertainment, including a Golden Tee golf simulator and Buck Hunter gaming system, as well as foosball and shuffleboard. The post PCCP Provides $51.8 Million Refi of Gilbert 236-Unit Multifamily Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PCCP refinances Everly at Morrison Ranch (Gilbert, AZ): 236-unit Class A, $51.8M senior loan
Built 2025, 97% leased; 36 two-story buildings, 986 SF avg unit, mix of 1/2/3-bed with full amenity suite
Loan backs newly built, lease-up-phase garden-style product in master-planned suburban Phoenix community
stories.summary: PCCP provided a $51.8M senior refinance for Everly at Morrison Ranch, a 236-unit Class A garden-style community in Gilbert, Arizona, built in 2025 and currently 97% leased. The property features 36 two-story buildings with an average unit size of 986 SF, premium amenities (pool/spa, fitness center, gaming areas), and sits within the master-planned Morrison Ranch neighborhood.
stories.impact: Gilbert lease-up velocity (97% occupancy in first year post-delivery) validates Phoenix suburban multifamily demand amid population influx from California. Refi at this stage signals strong underwriting metrics and lender appetite for Arizona garden-style; watch for rent growth sustainability as supply deliveries accelerate in Phoenix-Metro (Tempe, Chandler, Gilbert submarkets).

stories.title: JLL Secures $617M Financing for Grubb Properties Apartment Tower in Lower Manhattan
stories.excerpt: JLL’s M&A and Corporate Advisory, Corporate Banking Advisory and Debt & Equity Advisory teams collectively secured $617 million in financing for Link Apartments REIT and Link Apartments Opportunity Zone REIT. Both are Grubb Properties-managed REITs, along with Link Apartments 8 Carlisle, a 64-story, Class A multifamily development in Manhattan’s Financial District. JLL represented the developer and REIT sponsor, Grubb Properties, in arranging the multi-tranche capitalization through a coordinated, three-phase advisory effort. It included the following components: First, the M&A and Corporate Advisory group, led by senior managing director Steve Hentschel and director Adam Coleman, advised on the merger of multiple legacy Grubb funds and subsequent rebranding to create Link Apartments REIT. Led by senior managing director Anthony Fertitta and associate Jonathan Koletic, the Corporate Banking Advisory group advised Link Apartments REIT and Link Apartments Opportunity Zone REIT on securing a $240-million NAV credit facility from Bayview Commercial Mortgage Finance, supporting the consolidation of the 45-property portfolio while providing an equity commitment to assist in capitalizing Link Apartments 8 Carlisle. Additionally, JLL’s Debt & Equity Advisory group, in conjunction with Arrow Real Estate Advisors, arranged a $300-million senior construction loan from Maxim Capital Group and a $77-million mezzanine loan that was co-originated by GreenBarn Investment Group, Skylight Real Estate Partners, Axonic Capital and Meadow Partners. The Debt & Equity Advisory team was led by managing director Stephen Van Leer, senior managing directors Rob Hinckley and Jeffrey Julien, managing director Steven Rutman and directors Alex Staikos and John Lowe. Join the industry’s top multifamily decision-makers at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Hear from the investors, developers, lenders, owners, and advisors shaping today’s apartment market throu
bullets: JLL arranges $617M financing for Link Apartments 8 Carlisle: 64-story FiDi tower, Grubb Properties developer
Multi-tranche structure: $240M NAV credit (Bayview), $300M senior construction (Maxim), $77M mezzanine (GreenBarn/Skylight/Axonic/Meadow)
Financing also consolidates 45-property Link Apartments portfolio; mezzanine co-originated by four institutional partners
stories.summary: JLL arranged $617M in multi-tranche financing for Link Apartments 8 Carlisle, a 64-story Class A multifamily tower in Manhattan's Financial District developed by Grubb Properties. The capital stack includes a $240M NAV credit facility from Bayview Commercial Mortgage, a $300M senior construction loan from Maxim Capital, and a $77M mezzanine loan co-originated by GreenBarn, Skylight, Axonic, and Meadow Partners. The deal also refinances a 45-property portfolio consolidation.
stories.impact: FiDi high-rise multifamily financing (four mezzanine partners, $617M total) signals strong institutional confidence in NYC downtown residential despite office-to-conversion supply glut. Multiple lenders sharing risk on single asset indicates heightened scrutiny of development execution and lease-up risk; watch for construction loan draws and leasing velocity as leading indicators of Lower Manhattan demand recovery.

stories.title: New York City Hotel Labor Deal Creates Hurdles for Hospitality Underwriting
stories.excerpt: The myriad headwinds facing New York City hotel investors just got even stiffer. The Big Apple’s hospitality industry confronted rising operating and borrowing costs from persistent inflation in recent years that was further compounded by a big drop in international tourism since President Donald Trump assumed office for his second term. Disappointing booking numbers for FIFA World Cup matches held at nearby MetLife Stadium in June and July dealt an additional blow. The latest financial obstacle might be the biggest and longest-lasting of all — and, in the process, create more barriers to obtaining necessary capital to keep the lights on. The Hotel Association of New York City (HANYC) reached a historic eight-year labor deal this spring covering over 27,000 workers at more than 200 hotels. Among other provisions, it puts housekeepers and non-tipped staff on track to earn over $100,000 annually by 2034. The agreement will make it that much more challenging for hotel owners to obtain financing as lenders tighten their underwriting standards to reflect the higher labor costs. “Labor can be up to 50 percent of your operating expenses, and even higher in a luxury property or a very service-intensive property,” said Solomon Garber, co-founder and chief revenue officer of Erithmitic, a tech-oriented lending platform focused on the hospitality industry. “The fixed labor costs, including wages, health care, pension and staffing requirements, can be up to 30 percent higher than the same hotel that is nonunion.” The HANYC contract that took effect July 1 will raise wages by more than 50 percent over the life of the agreement, which runs through June 30, 2034, according to a summary of the deal released by the Hotel and Gaming Trades Council, the union representing hotel workers. The added expenses to wages and benefits from the contract are projected to increase annual property costs for hospitality owners by 15 percent, according to HANYC. Garber noted that even a 10 percent
bullets: NYC hotel labor deal: 8-year agreement covers 27K workers at 200+ hotels; housekeeping to exceed $100K by 2034
Labor costs up to 50% of hotel operating expenses; deal tightens lender underwriting, raising financing barriers for hospitality
Compounded by inflation, tourism weakness post-Trump, FIFA World Cup booking shortfalls; financing availability deteriorating
stories.summary: The Hotel Association of New York City reached an historic eight-year labor agreement covering 27,000+ workers at 200+ hotels, putting housekeeping and non-tipped staff on track to earn over $100,000 annually by 2034. Labor represents up to 50% of hospitality operating expenses, and the deal is forcing lenders to tighten underwriting standards. NYC hospitality is also burdened by post-Trump tourism declines and disappointing World Cup bookings.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to core multifamily development, but important signal: NYC hotel NOI compression from labor costs may accelerate office-to-residential conversions as a capital preservation strategy. Higher hospitality financing costs could free up some development capital/lender capacity for multifamily, but intensifies competitive capital constraints across all NYC asset classes.

stories.title: NRP Advancing 336-Unit Austin Affordable Housing Venture
stories.excerpt: The NRP Group announced the financial closing and groundbreaking of Catalina, a 336-unit affordable housing community in Travis County near Austin, Texas. Developed in partnership with the Housing Authority of Travis County (HATC), the community will provide affordable housing for families and working professionals earning between 30% and 70% of the Area Median Income (AMI). Catalina will consist of 12, three-story residential buildings with a mix of one- to four-bedroom homes. Amenities include a swimming pool, 24-hour fitness center, business center, a multipurpose community room with a full kitchen, children’s activity room and playground. Supportive resident services will include after-school care, financial literacy courses, ESL classes, first-time homebuyer programs and more. Residents will also have access to a complimentary shuttle service. Tax credit equity was provided by Huntington Community Development Corporation. Construction and permanent financing is provided by PIMCO and arranged by Berkadia. Construction on Catalina is now underway with completion scheduled for April 2028. The post NRP Advancing 336-Unit Austin Affordable Housing Venture appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NRP Group closes Catalina: 336-unit affordable housing, Travis County near Austin; 30–70% AMI targeting
12 three-story buildings, one- to four-bed mix; PIMCO construction/permanent financing, Berkadia arranging
Supportive services: after-school care, ESL, financial literacy, first-time buyer programs; completion April 2028
stories.summary: The NRP Group announced financial closing and groundbreaking of Catalina, a 336-unit affordable housing community in Travis County near Austin, developed with the Housing Authority of Travis County. Units target households earning 30–70% AMI. The project includes 12 three-story buildings, full resident support services, and is financed via PIMCO (construction and permanent) with Berkadia arranging. Completion is scheduled for April 2028.
stories.impact: Austin metro affordable housing supply expanding via low-income tax credit (LIHTC) pipeline. Catalina's size (336 units) and PIMCO backing indicate institutional investor appetite for stabilized affordable product. Austin's rapid population growth and affordability crisis are driving LIHTC allocations; watch for competing supply/demand dynamics between market-rate and subsidized units in Travis County.

stories.title: U.S. Apartment Demand Outpaces New Supply for First Time Since 2022
stories.excerpt: U.S. apartment demand outpaced new supply over the past year for the first time since early 2022, Cushman & Wakefield said in its second-quarter 2026 U.S. Multifamily MarketBeat. Vacancy fell below 9% for the first time since 2024 and construction activity continued to retreat in Q2. Net absorption totaled 124,600 units during Q2, the fifth-highest quarterly total in nearly 25 years and an 8% year-over-year increase. National vacancy declined 35 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 8.9%, ending more than a year of relative stability. On a trailing four-quarter basis, approximately 362,000 units were absorbed compared with roughly 358,000 units delivered. “The apartment market is no longer defined by new supply pressures,” said Sam Tenenbaum, head of multifamily insights at Cushman & Wakefield. “Construction is slowing, demand has remained remarkably resilient and vacancy is now moving lower. That’s a meaningful shift from the conditions that shaped the market over the past several years and points to improving fundamentals as the pipeline continues to thin.” The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post U.S. Apartment Demand Outpaces New Supply for First Time Since 2022 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: US apartment demand outpaces supply for first time since early 2022; Q2 net absorption 124.6K units (fifth-highest in 25 years)
National vacancy fell 35 bps Q2 to 8.9%, below 9% for first time since 2024; trailing four-quarter absorption 362K vs. deliveries 358K
Construction activity slowing; supply pipeline thins; rental fundamentals inflecting from oversupply to tight conditions
stories.summary: For the first time since early 2022, US apartment demand outpaced new supply, according to Cushman & Wakefield's Q2 2026 MarketBeat. Net absorption totaled 124,600 units in Q2 (the fifth-highest quarterly total in nearly 25 years), while national vacancy declined 35 basis points to 8.9%—below the 9% threshold for the first time since 2024. On a trailing four-quarter basis, absorption of 362,000 units exceeded deliveries of 358,000 units.
stories.impact: Inflection point: supply/demand dynamics have flipped from oversupply to incipient tightness. Construction activity slowing, vacancy compressing, and strong absorption signal improving pricing power and rent growth acceleration ahead. Developers should focus on execution of near-term deliveries and position land/entitlements in supply-constrained, high-demand submarkets; cap rates likely to compress further if rent growth outpaces historical levels.

stories.title: Investment Firm T7 Capital Signs 4K-SF Lease at GFP’s 515 Madison Avenue
stories.excerpt: GFP Real Estate has signed three new deals at its Midtown office tower at 515 Madison Avenue, totaling nearly 9,000 square feet of space, the landlord announced Tuesday. In the largest deal, investment management firm T7 Capital signed a five-year, 3,956-square-foot lease on the building’s 29th floor. It’s unclear whether the deal represents a relocation or a new location for T7, which has a current office address listed at 1040 Avenue of the Americas on its website. Michael Beyda and Joel Kubie from Benchmark Group New York represented the tenant. The brokers did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Meanwhile, Innovation Training Specialist, a health care-related training provider, also inked a 10-year lease for 3,046 square feet on the fifth floor of 515 Madison, GFP announced. Max Vizgalin from OfficeNYC.com, who represented the tenant, did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Finally, Widowski Law Group signed a five-year extension and relocation within the building. The firm will move from the fifth floor to the 27th floor, where it will occupy 1,881 square feet. GFP worked directly with the law firm on this deal. Martin McGrath and Kephra Stone from GFP represented the landlord in all three deals. “These transactions demonstrate the continued appeal of 515 Madison to a wide range of businesses seeking high-quality office space in one of Manhattan’s premier business districts,” Stone said in a statement. “We’re seeing demand from both new tenants entering the building and existing tenants choosing to renew and expand their long-term relationship with us. Combined with our recent refinancing of the property and strong leasing velocity, these leases reinforce the building’s position as one of Midtown’s most sought-after boutique office destinations.” The asking rent for each deal was not disclosed, but the average asking rent for office space in the Plaza District was $95.71 p
bullets: T7 Capital signs 3,956 SF lease at GFP's 515 Madison Avenue (Midtown), 29th floor, five-year term
GFP closed three deals totaling ~9K SF at tower; also signed Innovation Training Specialist and Widowski Law Group extensions
Unclear if T7 Capital relocating from 1040 Avenue of the Americas or establishing new NYC footprint
stories.summary: GFP Real Estate announced three new office leases at 515 Madison Avenue in Midtown Manhattan totaling nearly 9,000 SF. Investment management firm T7 Capital signed the largest deal—a 3,956-SF, five-year lease on the 29th floor. Two other tenants, Innovation Training Specialist and Widowski Law Group, also signed new or extension leases. It is unclear whether T7 Capital is relocating or opening a new NYC office.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. Office leasing at Class A Midtown buildings suggests some flight-to-quality by financial services tenants (T7 Capital); however, limited data on whether this represents net office demand growth or just consolidation/relocation. Watch for sublease availabilities and churn as broader indicator of office-to-conversion pressure in NYC.

stories.title: Pfizer Incident or Not, New York Still Has a Lot of Office Conversions Left to Go
stories.excerpt: For a few days last week, two buckling steel columns at the Pfizer Building became the biggest real estate story in America. If you only read the headlines, you might conclude that office-to-residential conversions are suddenly in trouble. The opposite is closer to the truth. Quietly, New York has become the national leader in conversions. Approximately 70 projects with more than 20,000 potential units are underway, representing billions of dollars of investment and one of the fastest shifts in generations for how the city builds housing. Nate Bliss. Photo: Courtesy Latent Urban Ventures. Yet, remarkably little has been written about what is actually happening — or why. Over the past two years, office-to-residential conversions have gone from a trickle to a pour, and now represent a meaningful share of the multifamily pipeline. Thousands of units of affordable housing are being delivered in some of the wealthiest areas, including into buildings on Billionaires Row and Wall Street. Millions of square feet of defunct office space are being repurposed. And, despite a troubling week for the Pfizer building conversion, this trend is only accelerating. So how did New York City become the shining beacon for the office conversion revolution? Two game-changing moments occurred over the last two years. First, in April 2024, the governor and state legislature reached a landmark housing deal that included critical regulatory changes and a new incentive program called 467-m, designed to induce conversion projects and ensure that 25 percent of the resulting homes would be permanently affordable. The second moment came later that year, when the City Council passed City of Yes for Housing Opportunity, the most sweeping overhaul of zoning since the 1960s. For office conversions, City of Yes updated arcane rules around qualifying buildings and addressed regulatory incoherencies that had blocked past projects. What has resulted is one of the most effective housing production programs
bullets: NYC has ~70 office-to-residential conversion projects underway with 20K+ potential units; billions in investment deployed
Thousands of affordable units delivered in high-value areas (Billionaires Row, Wall Street); trend accelerating post-Pfizer building incident
Conversion pipeline represents major multifamily supply and affordability solution; most transformative housing shift in NYC in decades
stories.summary: Despite a brief media scare over the Pfizer Building's structural issues, NYC has quietly become the national leader in office-to-residential conversions. Approximately 70 active projects represent 20,000+ potential residential units and billions in investment. Thousands of affordable units are being delivered in prime neighborhoods including Billionaires Row and Wall Street. The trend is accelerating and represents one of the fastest shifts in how the city builds housing in generations.
stories.impact: NYC multifamily supply pipeline now substantially skewed toward conversion rather than new construction, reshaping submarkets and affordability profiles. Class B/C office buildings in Midtown, Downtown, and outer boroughs are economically viable conversion candidates if rents justify construction costs; lenders warming to conversions as de-risked vs. ground-up development. Expect continued conversion velocity and rental compression in CBD submarkets as supply hits market 2027–2029.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami luxury home: $18.9M sale of new 10K SF mansion in Ponce-Davis neighborhood; telecom exec buyer
Commercial: Moishe Mana-linked entity buys Wynwood buildings (13K SF) at 380/320 NW 26th for $24.5M; Pastis, Apparatus tenants
High-end residential and commercial activity in Miami signals investor confidence despite broader macro headwinds
stories.summary: Miami recorded two major transactions in July 2026: a $18.9M sale of a new 10,000-SF mansion in Ponce-Davis to a telecom executive, and a $24.5M commercial sale of Wynwood buildings (13,000 SF) by a Moishe Mana-linked entity with tenants Pastis and Apparatus. Both deals reflect active capital deployment in South Florida real estate at high price points.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance. Moishe Mana's continued Wynwood acquisition activity (mixed-use/retail) signals ongoing neighborhood densification, which could support multifamily nearby. Luxury residential sales volume indicates wealth inflows to Miami; watch for concurrent multifamily rent growth in premium neighborhoods (Brickell, Wynwood, Allapattah).

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing + Capstone Equities plan 316 affordable units (100% AMI-restricted) at 21155 Califa, Woodland Hills; demolish 35K SF office
Seven-story development on 2.7 acres; eligible for LA Executive Directive 1 expedited approval; tied to largest new permit issued early July
Kroenke Rams headquarters development (52 acres, $800M+) at 6100 Owensmouth nearby driving Warner Center affordability push
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities acquired 21155 Califa Street in Woodland Hills to develop 316 affordable units across seven stories, replacing a 35,000-SF commercial building. The project, called The Green at Warner Center, qualifies for LA's Executive Directive 1 expedited approval path as 100% affordable housing. The development sits within Warner Center, where LA Rams owner Stan Kroenke is advancing a 52-acre, mixed-use headquarters redevelopment.
stories.impact: Woodland Hills/Warner Center is rapidly becoming an affordability hub amid Stan Kroenke's $800M+ mixed-use investment and citywide affordable housing mandate push. Elysian's second large affordable conversion (prior 75K SF office-to-housing at Balboa Boulevard) signals institutional developer confidence in San Fernando Valley rent sustainability and policy support. Watch for office-to-conversion velocity and unit absorption in submarkets near anchor institutional anchors (sports/corporate HQ).

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill, TX (Austin suburb) selects Griffin Swinerton JV to lead downtown revamp; target groundbreaking 2028
Vision: walkable gathering spaces, historic preservation, district-specific zoning, parking upgrades; developer-as-extension-of-city-staff model
Liberty Hill tripled population since 2020 census; Griffin Swinerton handling 29-acre Pflugerville Downtown East and prior Austin suburb projects
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, one of the nation's fastest-growing Austin suburbs, selected the Griffin Swinerton joint venture (Griffin Structures + Swinerton) to lead a downtown revamp targeting 2028 groundbreaking. The developer will function as quasi-municipal staff, soliciting community input, evaluating infrastructure, and structuring financing for a walkable, historic-preservation-focused downtown with district-specific uses. Liberty Hill tripled in size since 2020.
stories.impact: Rapid Austin-area suburban population growth (Liberty Hill +300% since 2020) is driving public-private downtown activation partnerships. Developer-led infrastructure + mixed-use planning in fast-growing suburbs signals demand for walkable multifamily product in town centers over sprawl. Follow Griffin Swinerton's Downtown East (Pflugerville, 29 acres) and Liberty Hill execution for clues on suburban multifamily absorption and transit-oriented development feasibility.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK Companies secures piles permit for 910 South Wells, Chicago South Loop: 28-story, 350-unit residential, 3.3K SF retail
Part of truncated Riverline master plan (8 acres, originally 15 acres before Lendlease split in 2018); connected to riverwalk via pedestrian path
CMK secured $83M construction loan from Wintrust for first phase (388-unit building at 1010 South Wells); general contractor Brandt's Build
stories.summary: CMK Companies obtained piles permits for 910 South Wells Street, a 28-story mixed-use tower in Chicago's South Loop designed by Gensler with 350 residential units and 3,300 SF of retail. The building is part of CMK's Riverline development (eight acres), which split from a larger 15-acre master plan partnership with Lendlease in 2018. CMK also secured an $83M construction loan from Wintrust for the adjacent 388-unit tower at 1010 South Wells, which is already under construction.
stories.impact: Chicago South Loop is consolidating around dense residential-riverside development. CMK's stacked permitting and $83M construction financing signal strong lender confidence in Chicago CBD multifamily recovery post-remote-work. Watch construction timelines for 1010 and 910 South Wells as indicators of South Loop lease-up velocity and rent growth; Lendlease's completed Cooper and Reed towers provide comparable comps.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas ($800M wellness resort, 24 acres, 500K SF) seeks approval for pedestrian skybridge, new non-residential zones; 2027 groundbreaking target
Resort features thermal baths, saunas, restaurants, retractable glass roof, swim-up bars on Trinity River; site adjacent to Water Commons park
Company faces credibility questions: NY Times revealed misrepresentation of development experience (one spa vs. claimed six facilities globally)
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an Austrian wellness company, is seeking city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to its planned $800M, 500,000-SF resort on 24 acres along the Trinity River. Groundbreaking is targeted for 2027. The project includes thermal baths, saunas, restaurants, and a retractable glass roof creating a beach-like atmosphere. However, the New York Times revealed Therme misrepresented its development track record to Ontario authorities, claiming six global facilities under construction when only one spa had been completed.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance, but Therme's credibility issues could delay Trinity River waterfront district activation and suppress nearby residential demand. Larger signal: unfamiliar international developers entering US markets face regulatory scrutiny and public skepticism; unknown execution risk may reduce nearby land values and complicate mixed-use residential financing. Monitor for project delays or capital withdrawal.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development secures $28M mid-construction financing for The Albritton (Friendswood, TX): 111 units, 20K SF mixed-use
70% LTV loan from S3 Capital via Concord Summit; completion 2028; part of larger Tannos pipeline (Friendswood City Center $750M, Magnolia Town Center $1B)
Friendswood is 25 mi SE of Downtown Houston, halfway to Galveston; 42K residents; Tannos/Wolfgramm and Tannos/Maritia partnerships
stories.summary: Tannos Development secured $28M in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas, featuring 111 multifamily units, 11,000 SF of ground-floor retail, and 9,000 SF of office space. The 70% LTV loan from S3 Capital (arranged by Concord Summit) carries the project through completion in 2028. Tannos is also developing two much larger projects in the region: Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106 acres) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres).
stories.impact: Houston metro suburbs (Friendswood, Magnolia) are consolidating mixed-use development pipelines around Tannos and partnering entities. Friendswood mezzanine financing ($28M on $111 units = ~$250K/unit debt) signals healthy underwriting for suburban mixed-use. Watch for absorption velocity at The Albritton and City Center as leading indicators of Houston-area suburban multifamily demand; proximity to Galveston may create seasonal/tourism dynamics not typical to inland suburbs.
